records.narrative: The plan is flat at 672 units/week for 22 of 26 weeks — that's a placeholder, not a real projection. No retail account buys on a perfectly even cadence like that. Their actual buying pace over the last 13 weeks averages 300/week with normal week-to-week variation. A flat plan will either leave you short when they buy heavy or overstocked when they pull back.

records.narrative: The plan is flat at 40 units/week for 26 of 26 weeks — that's a placeholder, not a real projection. No retail account buys on a perfectly even cadence like that. Their actual buying pace over the last 13 weeks averages 33/week with normal week-to-week variation. A flat plan will either leave you short when they buy heavy or overstocked when they pull back. The plan is back-loaded — only 50% of the volume is in the first 13 weeks. But this account's buying pattern shows stronger near-term demand (66% front-half). If they pull early, you may not have the inventory to support it. The plan is significantly below what this account has been buying in 6 weeks: W1 (40 planned vs. 96 expected), W8 (40 planned vs. 132 expected), W9 (40 planned vs. 96 expected) and 3 more weeks. That's roughly 396 units of unplanned demand. If the account pulls at their normal pace, you could be looking at out-of-stocks or rushed replenishment orders.
